findings.title: Operator keypair stored as a file-mounted secret without rotation or HSM protection
findings.description: The settlement sidecar loads the operator keypair from a JSON file at `OPERATOR_KEYPAIR_PATH` (mounted via ExternalSecret). This keypair has authority to settle and refund any task — effectively the master billing key. If the pod is compromised, the attacker gains full control of the billing program. There is no mention of key rotation, HSM-backed signing (e.g., AWS KMS, GCP Cloud KMS), or multi-signature authority. The keypair is a raw JSON file on the filesystem.
findings.recommendation: 1. Use a KMS-backed signer (e.g., AWS KMS with solana-remote-wallet or a custom signing service) instead of mounting a raw keypair file. 2. If raw keypair is unavoidable for hackathon, ensure the filesystem is read-only (already planned in task 11) and restrict file permissions to 0400. 3. Post-hackathon: implement key rotation by supporting operator authority transfer on-chain (update OperatorConfig.authority) and add a key-rotation runbook. 4. Add a secondary approval mechanism (multisig or time-lock) for high-value settlements.

findings.title: Single operator keypair is a single point of compromise for all settlement and refund operations
findings.description: The on-chain program validates `operator.key() == operator_config.authority` for settle_task, refund_task, pause, and unpause. A single compromised key can settle fraudulent tasks, drain customer balances, refund to inflate balances, or pause/unpause the program. There is no multisig, no spending limits on the operator, and no time-delayed settlement.
findings.recommendation: For hackathon: document this as a known limitation. Post-hackathon: 1. Implement an on-chain multisig requirement for operations above a threshold (e.g., settlements > 100 USDC require 2-of-3 signatures). 2. Add an operator spending cap (analogous to customer caps). 3. Implement a settlement delay window (e.g., 10 minutes) during which customers can dispute, before funds are actually transferred.

findings.title: Operator keypair transmitted and stored as plaintext in ExternalSecret without encryption-at-rest verification
findings.description: Task 11 and 14 mount the operator keypair via ExternalSecret, but there is no explicit requirement that the Kubernetes secret is encrypted at rest (etcd encryption), that the ExternalSecret provider (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) enforces encryption, or that the secret value is never logged. Kubernetes secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted, by default.
findings.recommendation: 1. Verify etcd encryption is enabled in the cluster (`EncryptionConfiguration` with aescbc or secretbox provider). 2. Ensure the ExternalSecret source (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) uses envelope encryption. 3. Add a `SecretProviderClass` annotation or use CSI secret store driver for tighter lifecycle control. 4. Add a CI check that the keypair never appears in logs (grep for base58 patterns in log output).

findings.title: No input validation on string lengths for task_id, package_id, source_uri in on-chain program before PDA derivation
findings.description: While Anchor's `#[max_len]` attribute enforces maximum string storage sizes, the SHA-256 hashing of task_id and package_id for PDA derivation happens before account initialization. An attacker could craft extremely long strings that consume excessive compute units during SHA-256 hashing. Additionally, there is no validation that task_id and package_id contain only safe characters — arbitrary bytes could cause issues in downstream systems (CLI display, web dashboard, S3 key construction).
findings.recommendation: 1. Add explicit `require!(task_id.len() <= 64, ...)` checks in the instruction handler before calling hash_seed(), not relying solely on Anchor's max_len at serialization time. 2. Add character validation for task_id and package_id (e.g., alphanumeric + hyphens only) to prevent injection in downstream systems. 3. Validate source_uri is a valid URL format or at least does not contain control characters.

findings.title: Refund instruction allows operator to credit arbitrary customer balances from treasury without independent verification
findings.description: The refund_task instruction credits customer_balance.balance from treasury funds with no independent verification, no customer consent, and no limit. A compromised or malicious operator could: 1. Settle a large fake task (debiting a customer), 2. Refund it (crediting the customer from treasury). While this seems neutral, the flow could be exploited if combined with other attack vectors, or the operator could selectively refund to inflate specific customer balances.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a refund cap: limit refunds to the original settlement amount (already done via task_receipt.amount, but verify no overflow). 2. Add a refund time window: refunds only allowed within N hours of settlement. 3. Log refund events prominently for audit. 4. Post-hackathon: implement customer-initiated dispute flow rather than operator-only refunds.

findings.title: No dependency version pinning or audit task for Rust crates and npm packages
findings.description: Neither the expanded_tasks nor scale_tasks include a task for pinning dependency versions (Cargo.lock, package-lock.json), running `cargo audit` or `npm audit`, or scanning for known vulnerabilities. The Anchor and Solana SDK ecosystems have had security advisories. The settlement sidecar Dockerfile uses `rust:1.78` without a digest pin.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add `cargo audit` and `npm audit` as CI steps (could be part of task 9/CI setup). 2. Pin Docker base images by digest (e.g., `rust:1.78@sha256:...`). 3. Pin all Cargo.toml dependency versions to exact versions or narrow ranges. 4. Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs. 5. Add `cargo deny check` for license and advisory compliance.

findings.title: Settlement sidecar container image not scanned for vulnerabilities
findings.description: Task 5006 creates a Dockerfile but there is no container image scanning step. The runtime image (debian:bookworm-slim) may contain known CVEs. The scale_tasks mention building via CI pipeline (task 14) but no scanning.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a Trivy or Grype scan step in the CI pipeline after image build, failing on CRITICAL/HIGH CVEs. 2. Use a distroless base image (gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12) instead of debian:bookworm-slim to minimize attack surface. 3. Set the image to run as non-root user in the Dockerfile (not just via PodSecurityStandard).

findings.title: Receipt JSON stored in S3/MinIO may contain PII (customer pubkey, transaction amounts) without encryption in transit to storage
findings.description: Task 4 uploads receipt JSON to S3 containing customer_pubkey, amounts, and agent information. Task 12 configures SSE-S3 encryption at rest, but the upload path from cto-controller to MinIO may not use TLS if the MinIO service is cluster-internal HTTP. Customer pubkeys are pseudonymous but could be linked to real identities.
findings.recommendation: 1. Ensure the MinIO endpoint URL uses HTTPS (TLS) even for in-cluster communication, or verify that the cluster network is encrypted (e.g., WireGuard via Cilium). 2. Consider encrypting receipt JSON at the application level before upload (envelope encryption with a KMS key) for defense in depth. 3. Add a data classification label to receipts indicating they contain financial PII. 4. Ensure S3 bucket access logs are enabled for audit trail.

findings.title: No on-chain audit trail for operator actions beyond TaskReceipt PDAs
findings.description: While TaskReceipt PDAs provide a settlement record, there is no comprehensive on-chain audit log for administrative actions (pause/unpause, operator config changes). Anchor events are emitted but are ephemeral — they exist only in transaction logs and can be pruned by validators. If an operator is compromised, reconstructing the timeline of malicious actions depends on Solana transaction history retention.
findings.recommendation: 1. Index all Anchor events to a persistent off-chain store (e.g., a dedicated event indexing service or use Helius/Shyft webhooks). 2. Add a PDA-based audit log for critical admin actions (pause/unpause, authority changes) as on-chain accounts. 3. Set up Datadog alerts (task 13) for pause/unpause events, unusual settlement patterns, and authority changes.

findings.title: Web dashboard has no authentication layer — any connected wallet can view all settlement data
findings.description: The web dashboard (tasks 7-8) fetches all TaskReceipt and AgentPackage accounts using `program.account.*.all()` with no access control. While on-chain data is inherently public, the dashboard provides a convenient aggregated view that could expose business intelligence (settlement volumes, customer spending patterns, agent earnings) to competitors.
findings.recommendation: For hackathon: acceptable as demo. Post-production: 1. Add wallet-based authentication that restricts the dashboard to authorized operator/admin wallets. 2. Filter data display to only show the connected user's own settlements and balance. 3. Add server-side API routes (Next.js API routes) that validate wallet signatures before returning aggregated data.

findings.title: Web dashboard deposit/withdraw dialogs lack client-side amount validation beyond min/max
findings.description: Task 8003 implements deposit/withdraw dialogs with basic validation (amount ≤ balance for withdrawals) but does not mention: integer overflow protection when converting float input to u64 lamports, validation against extremely small amounts (dust attacks), or protection against rapid repeated submissions (double-spend at UI level).
findings.recommendation: 1. Validate that the input amount, when multiplied by 10^6 for lamports conversion, does not exceed u64 max. 2. Set a minimum deposit/withdrawal amount (e.g., 0.01 USDC). 3. Disable the submit button immediately on click and re-enable only after transaction confirmation or failure (prevent double-submit). 4. Add a confirmation step for large amounts (e.g., > 100 USDC).

findings.title: Redis stream cto:settlements contains settlement instructions processable by any consumer with Redis access
findings.description: The Redis stream `cto:settlements` contains full settlement event payloads (customer pubkey, amounts, quality decisions) that, if read by an unauthorized consumer, could leak business data. More critically, if an attacker can XADD to this stream, they could inject fraudulent settlement events that the sidecar would process and submit on-chain.
findings.recommendation: 1. Task 9 already plans a NetworkPolicy restricting Redis access — ensure it is a hard requirement, not optional. 2. Configure Redis AUTH password and require it for all connections. 3. Consider signing settlement events (HMAC with a shared secret) so the sidecar can verify event integrity before processing. 4. Set Redis ACLs to restrict XADD to cto-controller only and XREADGROUP to settlement-sidecar only.

findings.title: Agent package registration is permissionless — no vetting of agent authors
findings.description: The register_agent_package instruction is permissionless (any wallet can register). While this enables an open marketplace, it means malicious actors could register packages with misleading package_ids or source_uris (e.g., phishing URLs, offensive content). The source_uri is displayed in the web dashboard without sanitization.
findings.recommendation: 1. Sanitize source_uri display in the web dashboard — use rel='noopener noreferrer' for links and validate URL format before rendering as clickable. 2. Consider adding an operator approval step for new packages (active=false by default, operator sets active=true after review). 3. Add content-length limits and basic URL format validation on source_uri on-chain.

findings.title: Receipt hash is operator-provided with no independent verification
findings.description: The settle_task instruction accepts receipt_hash as an argument from the operator. This hash is stored on-chain but never verified against actual receipt content by the program. The operator could submit arbitrary hashes that don't correspond to real receipts, undermining the integrity of the audit trail.
findings.recommendation: For hackathon: document as a trust assumption (operator is trusted). Post-hackathon: 1. Have the receipt uploaded to IPFS/Arweave with content-addressable storage, and verify the hash matches. 2. Implement a challenge period where customers can dispute receipt hashes. 3. Consider using Solana's recent blockhash or a timestamp oracle to prove receipt freshness.

findings.title: Settlement sidecar health endpoint (:8080/healthz) may expose internal state
findings.description: Task 5005 returns JSON including redis connectivity and solana RPC reachability status from the health endpoint. If this endpoint is exposed externally (task 10 mentions potential external monitoring), it could leak infrastructure topology information.
findings.recommendation: 1. Keep the healthz endpoint cluster-internal only (ClusterIP service, no Ingress). 2. If external monitoring is required, use a simple 200/503 response without the detailed JSON body for external probes, and expose the detailed response only on a separate internal port or path. 3. Add the endpoint to the NetworkPolicy allowlist only for monitoring pods.

findings.title: Daily spending cap reset uses unix_timestamp division which can be manipulated by validators
findings.description: The daily cap rollover logic computes `current_day = clock.unix_timestamp / 86400`. Solana validators have some flexibility in the Clock sysvar unix_timestamp (it can drift). While the drift is small, a malicious validator could potentially manipulate the timestamp to prematurely reset a customer's daily spending cap, allowing more spending than intended.
findings.recommendation: For hackathon: acceptable risk — validator timestamp manipulation requires significant stake. Post-hackathon: 1. Consider using slot-based time windows instead of unix_timestamp. 2. Add a minimum gap between daily resets (e.g., at least 23 hours must have passed since last reset). 3. Document the trust assumption that validator timestamps are approximately correct.

findings.title: No rate limiting on Solana RPC calls from web dashboard
findings.description: The web dashboard makes direct Solana RPC calls from the browser (via @solana/web3.js Connection) including fetching all TaskReceipt and AgentPackage accounts. This could be abused to amplify RPC costs (if using a paid provider) or hit rate limits, causing DoS for legitimate users.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add client-side rate limiting and caching (e.g., React Query/SWR with stale-while-revalidate). 2. Consider proxying RPC calls through a Next.js API route that can enforce rate limits per IP/wallet. 3. Use a dedicated RPC endpoint with per-origin rate limiting rather than the public devnet endpoint for production.

strengths: Feature-gating the billing code behind `solana-billing` Cargo feature flag is excellent — prevents billing dependencies from increasing attack surface of the core controller when not needed
PDA-based account derivation using deterministic SHA-256 seeds provides strong idempotency guarantees — duplicate task_id settlements are prevented at the protocol level
The fire-and-forget pattern for the controller's billing hook ensures the core reconciliation loop cannot be blocked or crashed by billing failures
Settlement event architecture with Redis streams provides clean decoupling — the controller never directly interacts with Solana RPC, reducing the blast radius of RPC failures
Comprehensive error enum with specific variants (ExceedsPerTaskCap, ExceedsDailyCap, etc.) enables precise error handling and avoids catch-all error swallowing
Network policies using CiliumNetworkPolicy with default-deny and explicit allowlists (task 11) is a strong zero-trust approach
The pause/unpause circuit breaker provides an emergency stop mechanism for the settlement engine
TaskReceipt PDAs serve as immutable on-chain audit records for every settlement and refund
Dead-letter queue routing for failed settlements prevents message loss while allowing manual investigation
Pod security standards (restricted profile) with non-root, read-only filesystem, and dropped capabilities (task 11) significantly reduces container escape risk
summary: The plan has a HIGH overall risk level primarily due to critical key management concerns around the operator keypair, which serves as a single point of compromise for all billing operations. The architecture is well-designed for the hackathon scope with good separation of concerns and defensive patterns (feature gating, fire-and-forget, circuit breaker), but production deployment requires addressing key management (SEC-001/002), Redis stream authentication (SEC-012), supply chain security (SEC-006), and container image scanning (SEC-007). The 17 findings are mostly addressable at the planning stage — 3 require immediate task modifications, 7 should be added as explicit subtasks, and 7 can be documented as post-hackathon improvements.
